question: What is the structure of a `charmcraft.yaml` file for a Kubernetes charm? Show a complete example with all required fields.

claude_output.result:
Here's a complete breakdown of the `charmcraft.yaml` file structure for a Kubernetes charm, along with a full example.

---

## Required Fields

| Field | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `type` | `string` | Must be `charm` |
| `name` | `string` | Charm identifier (determines `.charm` filename) |
| `base` | `string` | OS for building/running, e.g. `ubuntu@24.04` |
| `platforms` | `mapping` | Target architectures |
| `summary` | `string` | One-line description (max 78 chars) |
| `description` | `string` | Extended description (multiline OK) |
| `parts` | `mapping` | Build instructions (use the `charm` plugin) |
| `containers` | `mapping` | **(K8s-specific)** Workload container definitions |
| `resources` | `mapping` | **(K8s-specific)** One `oci-image` per container |

## Complete Example

```yaml
# === Core Identity ===
name: my-k8s-app
type: charm
title: My Kubernetes App
summary: A robust Kubernetes charm for My App
description: |
  This charm deploys and manages My App on any Kubernetes
  cloud supported by Juju. It provides high-availability,
  automated configuration, and integrations with common
  infrastructure services.

# === Base & Platforms ===
base: ubuntu@24.04
platforms:
  amd64:
    build-on: [amd64]
    build-for: [amd64]

# === Build Configuration ===
parts:
  charm:
    plugin: charm
    charm-entrypoint: src/charm.py        # default
    charm-requirements:
      - requirements.txt

# === Charm Libraries ===
charm-libs:
  - lib: data_platform_libs.data_interfaces
    version: "0"

# === Feature Requirements ===
assumes:
  - juju >= 3.1
  - k8s-api

# === Containers (K8s-specific) ===
containers:
  my-app:
    resource: my-app-image
    mounts:
      - storage: data
        location: /var/lib/my-app

# === OCI Image Resources (K8s-specific) ===
resources:
  my-app-image:
    type: oci-image
    description: OCI image for My App

# === Charm User Privilege (K8s, Juju 3.6+) ===
charm-user: non-root

# === Storage ===
storage:
  data:
    type: filesystem
    description: Persistent data store
    minimum-size: 1G

# === Relations ===
provides:
  website:
    interface: http
    description: HTTP endpoint for the web application

requires:
  database:
    interface: postgresql_client
    limit: 1
  ingress:
    interface: ingress
    limit: 1

peers:
  replicas:
    interface: my-app-replica

# === Configuration ===
config:
  options:
    app-port:
      type: int
      default: 8080
      description: Port on which the application listens
    log-level:
      type: string
      default: info
      description: "Logging verbosity (debug, info, warning, error)"
    enable-tls:
      type: boolean
      default: false
      description: Whether to enable TLS termination

# === Actions ===
actions:
  rotate-credentials:
    description: Rotate the application's internal credentials
  backup:
    description: Create a backup of the application data
    params:
      destination:
        type: string
        description: S3-compatible URI for the backup
    required:
      - destination

# === Metadata & Links ===
links:
  documentation: https://example.com/docs/my-k8s-app
  source:
    - https://github.com/example/my-k8s-app
  issues:
    - https://github.com/example/my-k8s-app/issues

# === Analysis Overrides (optional) ===
analysis:
  ignore:
    linters:
      - language
```

## Key Notes for Kubernetes Charms

1. **`containers` + `resources` are mandatory** — Every K8s charm must declare at least one container with a matching `oci-image` resource. The `resource` key inside the container definition must reference a name defined under `resources`.

2. **`assumes: [k8s-api]`** — Strongly recommended. This tells Juju the charm requires a Kubernetes backing cloud, preventing accidental deployment on machine models.

3. **`charm-user`** — Available since Juju 3.6. Controls the privilege level for hook execution:
   - `root` — hooks run as root (default, legacy behavior)
   - `sudoer` — hooks run as a non-root user with sudo access
   - `non-root` — hooks run as unprivileged user (most secure)

4. **`base` replaces `bases`** — The older `bases` key (with its long-form/short-form syntax) is deprecated. Use `base` + `platforms` instead.

5. **`parts.charm.plugin: charm`** — This is the standard plugin for ops-framework charms. It automatically packages `src/charm.py` and installs dependencies from `requirements.txt`.
